title: Entangling Superconducting Qubits via Energy-Selective Local Reservoirs
authors: Qihao Guo, Botao Du, Ruichao Ma
abstract: Engineered dissipation provides a powerful route to controlling and stabilizing quantum states in open systems. Superconducting circuits are particularly suited to this approach due to their tunable coupling to dissipative environments. Here we realize programmable local reservoirs for superconducting qubits through parametrically driven coupling to readout resonators, creating energy-selective incoherent pump and loss. Using coupled superconducting qubits, we autonomously stabilize entangled single-excitation states with fidelity up to 90.8%. We probe the stabilization dynamics under varying initial conditions and bath parameters, and implement robust classical shadow estimation for accurate and scalable state characterization. Finally, we numerically study a configuration where the engineered pump and loss share a common dissipative mode, leading to reservoir-mediated interference and classically correlated steady states. Our results demonstrate a scalable and hardware-efficient framework for dissipative preparation and control of correlated many-body states in superconducting circuits.
summary: The paper demonstrates a method to stabilize entangled states in superconducting qubits by engineering local energy-selective reservoirs using parametric drives. It also introduces a noise-resilient classical shadow estimation technique for efficient state characterization. This provides a scalable pathway for preparing correlated many-body states in circuit QED.
main_problem: Developing a scalable, hardware-efficient framework for the dissipative preparation and stabilization of entangled many-body states in superconducting circuits.
main_result: The researchers successfully stabilized entangled single-excitation Bell states with fidelities up to 90.8% using engineered local reservoirs and implemented a robust classical shadow estimation method for scalable state characterization.
method: Engineered dissipation via parametric sideband coupling to readout resonators, combined with robust classical shadow estimation and quantum state tomography for state verification.
model_or_system: A 1D array of frequency-tunable transmon qubits coupled to local readout resonators, realizing a hard-core Bose-Hubbard model (equivalent to an XY spin model) with engineered pump and loss reservoirs.
key_observables: State fidelity, purity, eigenstate populations, Pauli observables, and transverse correlations.
important_parameters: Tunneling J = 2pi x 6 MHz, interaction U = 2pi x -250 MHz, pump/loss coupling g_S/g_D approx 2pi x 0.6-0.7 MHz, and resonator linewidth kappa_r = 2pi x 1.5 MHz.
main_assumptions: Truncation of qubits to two levels, application of the rotating-wave approximation, and neglect of pure dephasing due to strong coherent interaction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the lattice implementation; Figure 4/5/6 display state tomography, population dynamics, and the performance/error analysis of robust classical shadow estimation.
paper_structure: Introduction to engineered dissipation, description of the hardware implementation and reservoir engineering, demonstration of Bell state stabilization, validation of robust shadow estimation, and exploration of shared reservoir interference effects.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates a practical way to use controlled dissipation to drive a system into a target many-body state, which is highly relevant for open quantum systems and the study of non-equilibrium phase transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Two-qubit dissipative stabilization. (a) For two res- onantly coupled qubits, a narrow-band incoherent pump se- lectively prepares a single-excitation eigenstate, as illustrated in the energy-level diagram. Solid arrows indicate transitions resonant with the pump for preparing |−⟩, and dotted arrows indicate unwanted off-resonant transitions. (b) Measured fi- delity to |−⟩as a function of pump duration, starting from initial state |gg⟩. (c) Measured eigenstate populations, show- ing leakage into |ee⟩at longer times. Dashed lines in (b,c) are numerical simulations using experimentally measured sys- tem parameters. Each experiment is typically averaged over 90,000 single-shot...

FIG. 3. Stabilization with pump and loss. (a) Adding a narrow-band loss on the second qubit selectively removes leakage population and stabilizes the target state. (b) Mea- sured stabilization dynamics from different initial Fock states, showing convergence to a steady state with 90.8% fidelity to |−⟩within 2 µs. Inset: zoom-in of the short-time dy- namics. Dashed lines are numerical simulations using ex- perimentally measured parameters. Statistical error bars are smaller than the marker size. (c) Representative stabilized density matrix measured at t = 2.8 µs. (d) Fidelity versus pump and loss detunings at a stabilization duration of 3 µs, measured experimentally (left) and compared with...

FIG. 4. Comparison of quantum state tomography, stan- dard classical shadow, and robust shadow estimation. (a) Fidelity to the |−⟩state for the stabilization experiment in Fig. 3, starting from the initial state |gg⟩. Robust shadow es- timation agrees closely with quantum state tomography, while standard classical shadow estimation exhibits systematic de- viations. (b) State purity as a function of stabilization dura- tion. See Appendix E for details on data analysis and error estimation.

FIG. 5. Interference effects from incoherent pump and loss implemented through a shared dissipative mode. Results are obtained from numerical simulation. (a) Steady-state fidelity to |−⟩versus pump and loss detunings δS and δD applied simultaneously to the same qubit. (b,c) Eigenstate population dynamics and steady-state density matrix for mismatched detunings (configuration 1), showing stabilization of |−⟩. (d,e) For matched detunings (configuration 2), a Raman-like process stabilizes the separable X-state ρX with coherences in both the even- and odd-parity manifolds.

FIG. 6. Stabilization of the |+⟩State. (a) Illustration for the pump and loss detuning. (b) Dynamics of the fidelity to the target state, when the initial state is |gg⟩. The dashed line is the numerical simulation. Inset: Reconstructed density matrix of the stabilized steady-state at t = 2.5 µs.
